/home/runner/work/internal/internal/services/flux-branch-manager/uv.lock /home/runner/work/internal/internal/services/validating-admission-webhook/uv.lock Using CPython 3.13.12 error: The requested interpreter resolved to Python 3.13.12, which is incompatible with the project's Python requirement: `>=3.11, <3.13`. The following `requires-python` declarations do not permit this version: - dagster-internal-buildkite: >=3.10, <3.13 - internal: >3.10, <3.13 Error: Process completed with exit code 2.
∴ Thinking…
The user is showing me a CI/GitHub Actions log where a workflow is failing. The error is:
error: The requested interpreter resolved to Python 3.13.12, which is incompatible with the project's Python requirement: `>=3.11, <3.13`. The following `requires-python` declarations do not permit this version: - dagster-internal-buildkite: >=3.10, <3.13 - internal: >3.10, <3.13
The issue is that UV_PYTHON is being set to 3.13 in the setup action, but the project's requires-python constraint is <3.13, so Python 3.13 is not compatible.
The user wants me to fix this CI failure, so I need to look at the relevant configuration files to understand what's setting the Python version and adjust it to be compatible with the project's constraints.
⏺ The CI is failing because UV_PYTHON is set to 3.13 but the project's requires-python constrains to <3.13. Let me explore the relevant files to understand the fix needed.
⏺ Explore(Explore erk-remote-setup action) ⎿ Done (16 tool uses · 37.9k tokens · 46s) (ctrl+o to expand)
⏺ Explore(Explore requires-python constraints) ⎿ Done (17 tool uses · 41.5k tokens · 1m 13s) (ctrl+o to expand)
∴ Thinking…
Now I have a clear picture. The issue is:
1. .github/actions/erk-remote-setup/action.yml sets python-version: "3.13" when calling astral-sh/setup-uv@v5 2. The root pyproject.toml has requires-python = ">3.10,<3.13" 3. dagster-internal-buildkite has requires-python = ">=3.10, <3.13" 4. Many other packages also have <3.13 constraints
So the fix is simple: change the Python version in the erk-remote-setup action from 3.13 to 3.12.
Let me check the action file to confirm the exact location and make the necessary change.
⏺ Now let me read the action file to see the exact line that needs changing.
⏺ Read(.github/actions/erk-remote-setup/action.yml) ⎿ Read 63 lines
∴ Thinking…
The fix is straightforward: change python-version: "3.13" to python-version: "3.12" in line 32 of .github/actions/erk-remote-setup/action.yml.
The root pyproject.toml has >3.10,<3.13, meaning Python 3.12 is the highest allowed version.
Let me write the plan and exit plan mode.
⏺ The issue is clear. Line 32 of .github/actions/erk-remote-setup/action.yml sets python-version: "3.13", but the root pyproject.toml and dagster-internal-buildkite both require <3.13. Python 3.12 is the highest compatible version.
⏺ Updated plan ⎿ Wrote 20 lines to /Users/schrockn/.claude/plans/warm-snacking-wand.md 1 # Fix: Python version mismatch in erk-remote-setup action 2 3 ## Context 4 5 The `erk-remote-setup` GitHub Action is failing because it configures Python 3.13 via `astral-sh/setup-uv@v5`, but the project's `requ ires-python` constraints restrict to `<3.13`: 6 - Root `pyproject.toml` (`internal`): `>3.10,<3.13` 7 - `.buildkite/dagster-internal-buildkite`: `>=3.10, <3.13` 8 - Multiple other packages (compass-data, purina, product-operations, etc.) also cap at `<3.13` 9 10 Python 3.12 is the highest version compatible with all workspace packages. 11 12 ## Change 13 14 **File:** `.github/actions/erk-remote-setup/action.yml` (line 32) 15 16 Change `python-version: "3.13"` → `python-version: "3.12"` 17 18 ## Verification 19 20 Re-run any workflow that uses this action (plan-implement, pr-address, pr-rebase, learn, one-shot) and confirm the `setup-uv` step suc ceeds without the Python version incompatibility error.
∴ Thinking…
The hook wants me to display the plan and ask the user about what to do with it. Let me follow the instructions.
⏺ Here's the plan:
--- Fix: Python version mismatch in erk-remote-setup action
Context
The erk-remote-setup GitHub Action is failing because it configures Python 3.13 via astral-sh/setup-uv@v5, but the project's requires-python constraints restrict to <3.13: - Root pyproject.toml (internal): >3.10,<3.13 - .buildkite/dagster-internal-buildkite: >=3.10, <3.13 - Multiple other packages (compass-data, purina, product-operations, etc.) also cap at <3.13
Python 3.12 is the highest version compatible with all workspace packages.
Change
File: .github/actions/erk-remote-setup/action.yml (line 32)
Change python-version: "3.13" → python-version: "3.12"
Verification
Re-run any workflow that uses this action (plan-implement, pr-address, pr-rebase, learn, one-shot) and confirm the setup-uv step succeeds without the Python version incompatibility error.
---
⏺ User answered Claude's questions: ⎿ · 📋 Fix: Python version mismatch in erk-remote-setup action Current context: (wt:internal) (br:master)
What would you like to do with this plan? → Create a plan PR
⏺ Skill(/erk:plan-save) ⎿ Successfully loaded skill
∴ Thinking…
Let me follow the plan-save skill instructions step by step.
Step 1: Parse Arguments
No --plan-type or --objective flags provided. - PLAN_TYPE_FLAG = "" - OBJECTIVE_FLAG = ""
Step 2: Generate Branch Slug
The plan title is "Fix: Python version mismatch in erk-remote-setup action" Branch slug: "fix-python-version-erk-setup" (28 chars, good)
Step 3: Generate Plan Summary
"The erk-remote-setup GitHub Action sets Python 3.13 via setup-uv, but the workspace requires-python constraints cap at <3.13. This changes the configured Python version to 3.12, the highest compatible version."
Now I'm executing the save command to persist this plan.
⏺ Bash(erk exec plan-save --format json --session-id="7f5b504c-d52f-44f2-984a-3dbb730102ba" --branch-slug="fix-python-version-erk-setup" --summary="The erk-remote-setup GitHub Action sets Python 3.13 via setup-uv, but the workspace requires-python constraints cap at less than 3.13. This changes the configured Python version to 3.12, the highest compatible version.") ⎿ { "success": true, "plan_number": 21229, "plan_url": "https://github.com/dagster-io/internal/pull/21229", "title": "[erk-plan] Fix: Python version mismatch in erk-remote-setup action", "branch_name": "plnd/fix-python-version-erk-set-03-02-1309", "plan_backend": "planned_pr", "objective_issue": null, "archived_to": "/Users/schrockn/code/internal/.erk/scratch/sessions/7f5b504c-d52f-44f2-984a-3dbb730102ba/plan_snapshots/000001-898c3e0f" } ⎿ (timeout 1m)

session.tool_result:
Perfect! Now I have all the information needed. Let me compile my findings:
## Summary
I've completed a comprehensive search of all GitHub Actions workflows that use the `erk-remote-setup` action. Here are my findings:
### 1. Workflows Using `erk-remote-setup`
**All 6 workflows found use the action without passing a `python-version` input:**
1. **`/Users/schrockn/code/erk/.github/workflows/one-shot.yml`** (line 91) 2. **`/Users/schrockn/code/erk/.github/workflows/pr-rewrite.yml`** (line 52) 3. **`/Users/schrockn/code/erk/.github/workflows/plan-implement.yml`** (line 117) 4. **`/Users/schrockn/code/erk/.github/workflows/pr-rebase.yml`** (line 57) 5. **`/Users/schrockn/code/erk/.github/workflows/pr-address.yml`** (line 44) 6. **`/Users/schrockn/code/erk/.github/workflows/learn.yml`** (line 55)
All 6 workflows call the action identically with no python-version parameter: ```yaml - uses: ./.github/actions/erk-remote-setup with: erk-pat: ${{ secrets.ERK_QUEUE_GH_PAT }} anthropic-api-key: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }} claude-code-oauth-token: ${{ secrets.CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN }} ```
### 2. The `erk-remote-setup` Action Definition
**File:** `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/.github/actions/erk-remote-setup/action.yml`
... [31 lines omitted] ...
session.thinking:
Now I have a clear picture. The erk repo's pyproject.toml says `>=3.11`, but the CI log the user showed is from the `internal` repo which has `<3.13`. The action is used in 6 workflows, none pass python-version.
The fix is to: 1. Add a `python-version` input with a default to the action 2. Use that input in the setup-uv step
The question is what the default should be. Since this is the erk repo and requires-python is >=3.11, but the action is used in CI for the `internal` repo too (the error was from `internal`). Let me re-read the CI log - it says `/home/runner/work/internal/internal/` so this action is being used from the internal repo context.
Looking at the CI output more carefully, I see the constraints are `>=3.10, <3.13` for both the buildkite and internal repos, so the action needs to work across different monorepos with varying Python requirements. I'll add a `python-version` input with a sensible default like "3.12" and reference it in the setup-uv step, then workflows can override it if needed.
session.assistant: Good context. The erk repo itself allows `>=3.11`, but when this action runs in the `internal` monorepo context, the `<3.13` constraints from dagster packages cause the failure. Let me write the plan.

"Skip PR and implement here" - Skip creating a PR, implement directly in current worktree (for small PR iterations that don't need PR tracking). 3. "View/Edit the plan" - Open plan in editor to review or modify before deciding.
⚠️ WARNING: Currently on 'master'. We strongly discourage editing directly on the trunk branch. Consider saving the plan and implementing in a dedicated worktree instead.
If user chooses 'Create a plan PR': 1. Run /erk:plan-save 2. STOP - Do NOT call ExitPlanMode. The plan-save command handles everything. Stay in plan mode and let the user exit manually if desired.
If user chooses 'Skip PR and implement here': 1. Create implement-now marker (skip PR creation): erk exec marker create --session-id be49c58d-ca80-4e9b-8eb6-a2a94caeba05 \ exit-plan-mode-hook.implement-now 2. Call ExitPlanMode 3. After exiting plan mode, implement the changes directly
... [7 lines omitted] ...
session.assistant:
Here's the plan:
---
## Make Python version configurable in erk-remote-setup action
### Context
The `erk-remote-setup` GitHub Action hardcodes `python-version: "3.13"` when calling `astral-sh/setup-uv@v5`. This fails when the action runs in repos with stricter Python constraints (e.g., the `internal` monorepo requires `<3.13`). The version needs to be configurable so each workflow/repo can specify what works for its dependency graph.
### Change
**File:** `.github/actions/erk-remote-setup/action.yml`
1. Add a `python-version` input with default `"3.12"`: ```yaml python-version: description: "Python version for uv" required: false default: "3.12" ```
2. Reference the input in the setup-uv step: ```yaml - uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@v5 with: python-version: ${{ inputs.python-version }} ```
No workflow file changes needed — all 6 callers (one-shot, pr-rewrite, plan-implement, pr-rebase, pr-address, learn) will pick up the default `3.12` automatically. Any caller can override by passing `python-version: "3.11"` etc.
### Verification
Re-run any workflow that uses this action and confirm the `setup-uv` step resolves to Python 3.12 and succeeds.
---

tool_use.name: AskUserQuestion
tool_use.param: [{'question': '📋 Make Python version configurable in erk-remote-setup action\nCurrent cont...[truncated 443 chars]...ion': 'Open plan in editor to review or modify before deciding.'}], 'multiSelect': False}]

session.tool_result:
User has answered your questions: "📋 Make Python version configurable in erk-remote-setup action Current context: (wt:erk) (br:master)
What would you like to do with this plan?"="Create a plan PR (Recommended)". You can now continue with the user's answers in mind.
session.user:
# /erk:plan-save
Save the current session's plan to GitHub with session context.
## Usage
```bash /erk:plan-save # Standalone plan /erk:plan-save --plan-type=learn # Learn plan (erk-learn label) /erk:plan-save --objective=123 # Explicit objective link (overrides marker) ```
Objective linking: if a plan was created via `/erk:objective-plan`, the session's objective-context marker is read automatically by the save command. Use `--objective=<number>` to override the marker or provide the link when no marker exists (e.g., during replan).
## Plan Storage
The plan is saved using the configured backend:
- **Draft PR backend** (`PLAN_BACKEND = "draft_pr"`): Creates a branch, pushes a plan commit, and opens a draft PR. Plan content is in the PR body after the metadata separator. The JSON output contract includes `plan_number`, `plan_url`, `title`, `branch_name`, `plan_backend`.
## Agent Instructions
### Step 1: Parse Arguments
Check `` for the `--plan-type` and `--objective` flags:
``` If contains "--plan-type=<type>": - Extract the type (standard or learn) - Store as PLAN_TYPE variable - Set PLAN_TYPE_FLAG to "--plan-type=<type>" Else: - Set PLAN_TYPE_FLAG to empty string
If contains "--objective=<number>": - Extract the number - Set OBJECTIVE_FLAG to "--objective=<number>" Else: - Set OBJECTIVE_FLAG to empty string ```
### Step 2: Generate Branch Slug
Before saving, generate a branch slug from the plan title. Read the plan title from the plan file you wrote in plan mode (the first `# ` heading).
Generate a branch slug from the title:
- 2-4 hyphenated lowercase words, max 30 characters - Capture distinctive essence, drop filler words (the, a, for, implementation, plan) - Prefer action verbs: add, fix, refactor, update, consolidate, extract, migrate - Examples: "fix-auth-session", "add-plan-validation", "refactor-gateway-abc"
Store the result as `BRANCH_SLUG`.
### Step 3: Generate Plan Summary
Write a concise 2-3 sentence summary of the plan. This summary will be visible at the top of the PR description (above the collapsed full plan).
Guidelines:
- Focus on WHAT the plan does and WHY - Do not repeat the title - Plain text, no markdown headers or formatting - Avoid special shell characters (backticks, dollar signs) - Store as PLAN_SUMMARY
### Step 4: Run Save Command
Run this command with the session ID, branch slug, summary, and optional flags:
```bash erk exec plan-save --format json --session-id="be49c58d-ca80-4e9b-8eb6-a2a94caeba05" --branch-slug="${BRANCH_SLUG}" --summary="${PLAN_SUMMARY}" ${PLAN_TYPE_FLAG} ${OBJECTIVE_FLAG} ```
Parse the JSON output to extract `plan_number` for verification in Step 5.
If the command fails, display the error and stop.
### Step 5: Verify Objective Link (if applicable)
**Only run this step if `objective_issue` is non-null in the JSON output from Step 4.**
Verify the objective link was saved correctly:
```bash erk exec get-plan-metadata <plan_number> objective_issue ```
Parse the JSON response:
- If `success: true` and `value` matches the expected objective number: verification passed - If `success: false` or value doesn't match: verification failed
**On verification success:**
Display: `Verified objective link: #<objective-number>`
**On verification failure:**
Display error and remediation steps:
``` ERROR: Objective link verification failed Expected objective: #<expected> Actual: <actual-or-null>
The plan was saved but without the correct objective link. Fix: Close draft PR #<plan_number>, ensure the objective-context marker exists, and re-run /erk:plan-save. ```
Exit without creating the plan-saved marker. The session continues so the user can retry.
### Step 6: Update Objective Roadmap (if objective linked)
**Only run this step if `objective_issue` was non-null in JSON output and verification passed.**
Update the objective's roadmap table to show that a plan has been created for this node:
1. **Read the roadmap node marker** to get the node ID:
```bash step_id=$(erk exec marker read --session-id "be49c58d-ca80-4e9b-8eb6-a2a94caeba05" roadmap-step) ```
If the marker doesn't exist (command fails), skip this step - the plan wasn't created via `objective-plan`.
2. **Update the roadmap table** using the dedicated command:
```bash erk exec update-objective-node <objective-issue> --node "$step_id" --pr "#<plan_number>" --status in_progress ```
This atomically fetches the objective body, finds the matching node row, sets the Status cell to `in-progress`, and writes the updated body back.
3. **Report the update:**
Display: `Updated objective #<objective-issue> roadmap: node <step_id> → plan #<plan_number>`
**Error handling:** If the roadmap update fails, warn but continue - the plan was saved successfully, just the roadmap tracking didn't update. The user can manually update the objective.
### Step 7: Display Results
**If JSON contains `skipped_duplicate: true`:**
Display: `Plan already saved as #<plan_number> (duplicate skipped)`
If `branch_name` is present in the JSON, display the same next-steps block as the success case below.
If `branch_name` is absent, display only: `View PR: <plan_url>`
Return immediately (skip Steps 5, 6 above if not already executed).
**Otherwise, on success**, display:
``` Plan "<title>" saved as draft PR #<plan_number> URL: <issue_url> ```
**Slot options block:**
The "OR exit Claude Code first" section should show both slot allocation options, with the recommended one listed first based on trunk detection:
If **on trunk = true**:
``` OR exit Claude Code first, then run one of:
New slot (recommended — you're on trunk): Local: erk br co --new-slot --for-plan <plan_number> Implement: source "$(erk br co --new-slot --for-plan <plan_number> --script)" && erk implement --dangerous
Same slot: Local: erk br co --for-plan <plan_number> Implement: source "$(erk br co --for-plan <plan_number> --script)" && erk implement --dangerous
Dispatch to Queue: erk pr dispatch <plan_number> ```
If **on trunk = false**:
``` OR exit Claude Code first, then run one of:
Same slot (recommended — you're in a slot): Local: erk br co --for-plan <plan_number> Implement: source "$(erk br co --for-plan <plan_number> --script)" && erk implement --dangerous
New slot: Local: erk br co --new-slot --for-plan <plan_number> Implement: source "$(erk br co --new-slot --for-plan <plan_number> --script)" && erk implement --dangerous
Dispatch to Queue: erk pr dispatch <plan_number> ```
**Next steps block:**
``` Next steps:
View PR: <plan_url>
In Claude Code: Dispatch to queue: /erk:pr-dispatch — Dispatch plan for remote agent implementation
OR exit Claude Code first, then run one of: Checkout: erk br co --for-plan <plan_number> Dispatch to Queue: erk pr dispatch <plan_number> ```
If objective was verified, also display: `Verified objective link: #<objective-number>`
If the JSON output contains `slot_name` and `slot_objective_updated: true`, also display: `Slot objective updated: <slot_name> → #<objective-number>`
**Note:** Slot objective updates are handled automatically by `plan-save` when an objective is linked via the session marker - no separate command call needed.
On failure, display the error message and suggest:
- Checking that a plan exists (enter Plan mode and exit it first) - Verifying GitHub CLI authentication (`gh auth status`) - Checking network connectivity
### Step 5: Push Planning Session
After saving successfully, push the planning session for cross-machine learning. This captures the in-progress session state so it's available even if implementation happens on a different machine.
Run `erk exec capture-session-info` to get the session file path, then push it:
```bash erk exec upload-impl-session --session-id "be49c58d-ca80-4e9b-8eb6-a2a94caeba05" 2>/dev/null || true ```
If the plan was saved successfully and `plan_number` is known, use push-session directly for better stage tracking:
```bash erk exec push-session \ --session-file "<session_file_path>" \ --session-id "be49c58d-ca80-4e9b-8eb6-a2a94caeba05" \ --stage planning \ --source local \ --plan-id <plan_number> \ 2>/dev/null || true ```
Where `<session_file_path>` is obtained from `erk exec capture-session-info` output and `<plan_number>` is from Step 2's JSON output.
**Note:** This is non-critical. If it fails, the plan was still saved successfully. The `2>/dev/null || true` ensures graceful degradation.
## Session Tracking
After successfully saving a plan, the plan number is stored in a marker file that enables automatic plan updates in the same session.
**To read the saved plan number:**
```bash erk exec marker read --session-id "be49c58d-ca80-4e9b-8eb6-a2a94caeba05" plan-saved-issue ```
This returns the plan number (exit code 0) or exits with code 1 if no plan was saved in this session.
session.assistant:
**Step 1: Parse Arguments** — No `--plan-type` or `--objective` flags provided.
**Step 2: Branch Slug** — `config-python-version-setup`
**Step 3: Plan Summary** — The erk-remote-setup action hardcodes Python 3.13, which is incompatible with repos constraining to less than 3.13. This adds a configurable python-version input defaulting to 3.12 so each caller can specify the version matching its dependency graph.

tool_use.name: Bash
tool_use.param: erk exec plan-save --format json --session-id="be49c58d-ca80-4e9b-8eb6-a2a94caeba05" --bra...[truncated 191 chars]... defaulting to 3.12 so each caller can specify the version matching its dependency graph."

session.tool_result: {"success": true, "plan_number": 8635, "plan_url": "https://github.com/dagster-io/erk/pull/8635", "title": "[erk-plan] Make Python version configurable in erk-remote-setup action", "branch_name": "plnd/config-python-version-setu-03-02-1332", "plan_backend": "planned_pr", "objective_issue": null, "archived_to": "/Users/schrockn/code/erk/.erk/scratch/sessions/be49c58d-ca80-4e9b-8eb6-a2a94caeba05/plan_snapshots/000001-7068d017"}
session.assistant: No objective linked — skipping Steps 5 and 6. Now pushing the planning session.

tool_use.name: Bash
tool_use.param: erk exec push-session \ --session-file "/Users/schrockn/.claude/projects/-Users-schroc...[truncated 94 chars]...\ --stage planning \ --source local \ --plan-id 8635 \ 2>/dev/null || true
